breakClass: .ToList()/.ToArray() (or a nested correlated-collection projection) embedded inside a server-side EF Core 10 query â€” either in a `let` clause whose value is consumed by a server `select new {...}`/`where ... .Contains(...)`, or as an inline `Field = (from ... let X=db....ToList() ... select ...).ToList()` sub-collection â€” on an outer IQueryable that has NOT yet crossed an .AsEnumerable()/.ToList() materialization boundary. EF6 client-evaluated these; EF Core 10 throws "could not be translated" (500). Outer queries that are materialized first (var data2 = v.ToList(); from o in data2 ...) or sourced from db.X.AsEnumerable() are SAFE.
why: db.PurchaseEntrys source with no materialization boundary; let app/AppStatus/chkAppStatus collection+GroupBy projections are server-side and untranslatable in EF Core 10. Two copies in this file (3062 and 3286). Port regression.
finalFix:
Mirror EF6's client-side evaluation by introducing an `.AsEnumerable()` materialization boundary BEFORE the `let` block, in BOTH copies (the GetPurchaseEntryPayment-family register grids at lines ~3050 and ~3274). Concretely, split the single LINQ query so the join+filter base runs server-side and the `let app/AppStatus/chkAppStatus/chkAppStatusme/sh` subqueries + final `select new {...}` run client-side:

1. Build the base query from `from a in db.PurchaseEntrys join ... from h in hir.DefaultIfEmpty()` through the `where ...` clause, projecting only the entity rows needed (e.g. `select new { a, b, c, d, g, i, h }`).
2. Apply `.AsEnumerable()` (or `.ToList()`) to that base query to cross the materialization boundary â€” exactly as done in the Payment/HireReturn fixes elsewhere in this audit.
3. Then run the `let app = db.AssignedTos...ToList()`, `let AppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates...ToList()`, and especially `let chkAppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates...GroupBy(...).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(...).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(x => x.ApprovalStatus).ToList()` and the final `select new {...}` over the materialized base sequence so they evaluate in-memory (LINQ-to-Objects), where GroupBy-first-per-group is fully supported.

This preserves identical output (each chkAppStatus subquery still keys by a.PurchaseEntryId / each row's PurchaseEntryId) while moving the untranslatable GroupBy projection client-side. Apply identically at line 3062 (GetPurchaseEntryPayment) and line 3286 (the second grid). Note these subqueries fire per base row (N+1) after the change â€” acceptable and matches EF6 behavior; if perf matters, the GroupBy results can be pre-fetched into a Dictionary keyed by (TransEntry) after the base materialization, but that is an optional optimization, not required for correctness.
reasoning:
I independently confirmed both criteria.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: The flagged `let` block (lines 3062-3068 first copy / 3286-3292 second copy) is inside `var v = (from a in db.PurchaseEntrys join ... select new {...}).ToList()...`. `db.PurchaseEntrys`, `db.ApprovalUpdates`, `db.AssignedTos` are real EF Core DbSets on ApplicationDbContext (ApplicationDbContext.cs:125/355/372). There is NO `.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToList()` boundary before the `select new {...}`; the FIRST materialization is the `.ToList()` at line 3125 (copy 1) / 3349 (copy 2). So the entire projection â€” app, AppStatus, and chkAppStatus â€” is composed into one server-side IQueryable and translated to SQL at that `.ToList()`. The `let` fragments are inside the boundary, not after it. Confirmed server-side. (The line-3286 copy's extra `where approveemp.Contains(empl.EmployeeId)` uses pre-computed C# locals, a constant boolean, and does not change translation.)

(b) WOULD 500: The decisive fragment is chkAppStatus: `db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(x => x.TransEntry==a.PurchaseEntryId && x.Type=="PurchaseEntryPayment").GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(x => x.ApprovalStatus).ToList()`. This is a correlated subquery (references a.PurchaseEntryId) projected as a collection into the server-side projection. `GroupBy(key).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(...).FirstOrDefault())` selects a WHOLE ENTITY (latest row) per group â€” a non-aggregate group projection. EF Core only translates GroupBy when the result selector is the key and/or aggregate operators (Count/Sum/Min/Max/Average); "first row per group" is not supported, and because it is nested in a server-side projection (subquery client-eval disabled since EF Core 3.0), EF Core 10 throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" -> HTTP 500. EF6 (legacy, identical code at _archive\legacy-mvc5\Controllers\PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs:3138-3213) tolerated this via its broader translation + client-side projection fallback, so this is a genuine PORT REGRESSION, not a pre-existing bug.

Severity HIGH: failure is at SQL translation time, independent of data â€” every call 500s. app/AppStatus alone are translatable correlated collection projections; chkAppStatus's GroupBy is what breaks the whole query. Both line-3062 and line-3286 copies are live (non-commented, reachable, return Json data at lines 3177/3401). The hunter's claim is validated.

breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new of unmaterialized Items/PEItemss/PurchaseEntrys join; .ToList() boundary at L8250 after projection -> server-side. Unconditional CLR helper on column -> untranslatable -> 500. Legacy (archive L9276) DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs line 8248 (method GetSerialNoDtlInCreateMode), replace:

    ExpiryDate  =   DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(c.PEDate, (int?)((a.daysexpirty != null)? a.daysexpirty : 360)),

with:

    ExpiryDate  =   c.PEDate.AddDays(a.daysexpirty ?? 360),

Rationale: c.PEDate is non-nullable DateTime (PurchaseEntry.cs:14), so no `.Value` is needed (and would not compile). a.daysexpirty is long? (Item.cs:43); `a.daysexpirty ?? 360` yields a non-null long that widens implicitly to the double parameter of DateTime.AddDays, preserving the legacy default of 360 days. EF Core 10 translates DateTime.AddDays(double) to SQL DATEADD(day, ...), exactly mirroring the legacy EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays(c.PEDate, ...) behavior. ExpiryDate stays a non-null DateTime, matching the prior output for every row (legacy DbFunctions.AddDays returned DateTime? but was never null here because PEDate is non-null and the days operand defaults to 360).
reasoning:
I independently inspected Controllers/PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs:8235-8252 (method GetSerialNoDtlInCreateMode). The query is a single LINQ-to-Entities expression: `from a in db.Items join b in db.PEItemss join c in db.PurchaseEntrys ... select new { ..., ExpiryDate = DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(c.PEDate, (int?)((a.daysexpirty != null)? a.daysexpirty : 360)), ... }).ToList()`. SERVER-SIDE: confirmed â€” the `.ToList()` materialization boundary is at line 8250, AFTER the `select new` projection (line 8243-8249). There is no `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToArray()` before the projection, and this is not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. So `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(...)` is part of the SQL-translated SELECT projection.

DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain CLR helper, NOT an EF-registered function: Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:70 defines `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`. EF Core 10 has no translation for this arbitrary static method, so at enumeration (.ToList()) the provider throws InvalidOperationException 'The LINQ expression ... could not be translated' â†’ HTTP 500. This fires on every path that returns rows (the projection always evaluates ExpiryDate), so it is high severity, not edge-only.

PORT REGRESSION: confirmed. Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs:8912 used the identical query with `DbFunctions.AddDays(c.PEDate, ...)` â€” the EF6 canonical function that translates to SQL DATEADD and ran fine. The port swapped DbFunctions.AddDays â†’ DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays, which breaks translation. So the hunter's classification is correct.

TYPE NOTE: the hunter's proposed fix `c.PEDate.Value.AddDays(...)` is wrong because PEDate is non-nullable DateTime (Models/PurchaseEntry.cs:14) â€” DateTime has no `.Value`. Also a.daysexpirty is `long?` (Models/Item.cs:43), not int. The corrected minimal fix below compiles and preserves the legacy 360-day default and DATEADD translation.

method: PurchaseEntryExpense grid action #1 (from a in db.PurchaseEntrys, terminates .ToList() at 3125)
breakClass: GroupBy-then-Select(g => g.OrderBy*/FirstOrDefault()) returning an ENTITY/row, used server-side (JOINed on g.FirstOrDefault(), or composed as a correlated 'latest-per-key' subquery in a non-materialized IQueryable). EF Core 10 cannot translate the grouped FirstOrDefault outside an aggregate context -> "could not be translated" runtime 500. Every finding has the identical shape in _archive/legacy-mvc5 where EF6 translated it, so each is a port regression.
why: Outer 'from a in db.PurchaseEntrys' not materialized before the let; chkAppStatus projected (3119), terminated by .ToList() (3125) -> server-side correlated GroupBy-latest subquery returning entities, untranslatable in EF Core 10 -> 500. The sibling 'let chkAppStatusme = ...Select(x=>x.ApprovedBy).FirstOrDefault()' on the next line is a SCALAR correlated FirstOrDefault and is FINE -- only the GroupBy variant breaks. Legacy has 3 occurrences of this let => port regression.
finalFix:
Mirror the established remediation in CreditSaleReturnNoteController.cs. Before the big `var v = (from a in db.PurchaseEntrys ...)` query (i.e. after line ~3048), materialize the approval rows once and build a client-side per-PurchaseEntry "latest status per ApprovedBy" lookup:

    // Materialize all PurchaseEntryPayment approval rows once, then compute latest-per-ApprovedBy client-side.
    var appUpdRows = db.ApprovalUpdates
        .Where(x => x.Type == "PurchaseEntryPayment")
        .Select(x => new { x.TransEntry, x.ApprovalStatus, x.ApprovedBy, x.CreatedDate })
        .ToList();
    var chkAppStatusLookup = appUpdRows
        .GroupBy(a => a.TransEntry)
        .ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g
            .GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy)
            .Select(grp => grp.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).First())
            .Select(a => a.ApprovalStatus).ToList());

Then DELETE the server-side `let chkAppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(...).GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()).ToList().Select(x => x.ApprovalStatus).ToList()` (lines 3064-3066) and the `chkAppStatus = chkAppStatus,` member at line 3119 from the server-side projection.

In the post-materialization `.ToList().Select(o => new {...})` block (line 3125+), inject the lookup. Since the `chkAppStatus` list is consumed at line 3152 in `ApprovalStatus = (... o.chkAppStatus.Count > 0 ...)`, restore it there. The simplest minimal edit: keep a server-projected key (the row already projects `a.PurchaseEntryId` as `o.PurchaseEntryId`), and in the outer Select compute:

    var chkAppStatus = chkAppStatusLookup.TryGetValue(o.PurchaseEntryId, out var ck) ? ck : new List<ApprovalStatus>();

then use `chkAppStatus` in place of `o.chkAppStatus` in the ApprovalStatus expression at line 3152.

This preserves identical output (latest ApprovalStatus per ApprovedBy for that PurchaseEntry, as List<ApprovalStatus>) and matches legacy EF6 semantics, while moving the untranslatable GroupBy-row projection to client memory. Apply the same fix to the two sibling occurrences in this file (the other GetPurchaseEntryPayment* grid actions that carry the identical `let chkAppStatus` at the legacy-mirrored positions). Do NOT use the proposed `.AsEnumerable()`-before-GroupBy approach â€” it does not create a valid client boundary inside a correlated server-side subquery.
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the flag is REAL (verdict=confirmed), not refuted.

SERVER-SIDE: Verified `db` is an `ApplicationDbContext` (EF Core; constructor at PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs:36-43, `using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore`). `db.PurchaseEntrys` and `db.ApprovalUpdates` are real DbSets. The query `(from a in db.PurchaseEntrys join... let chkAppStatus = ... select new {...})` is terminated by `.ToList()` only at line 3125 â€” there is NO `.AsEnumerable()`/`.ToList()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary before it, and the root `from a in db.PurchaseEntrys` is the IQueryable. So the entire projection (lines 3050-3124) is one server-side query tree EF Core 10 must translate. The `let chkAppStatus` (3064-3066) is a correlated subquery whose result is projected into the output member `chkAppStatus = chkAppStatus` at line 3119, so EF cannot defer it â€” it must build SQL for it.

WHY IT 500s: The subquery is `db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(x => x.TransEntry == a.PurchaseEntryId && x.Type == "PurchaseEntryPayment").GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault())...` â€” the classic "latest entire row per group" shape. EF Core's relational translator only supports a final `Select` over an IGrouping that projects AGGREGATES (Key/Count/Sum/Max...). Here `Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(...).FirstOrDefault())` projects a whole ROW per group, which EF Core 10 cannot translate -> throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" -> HTTP 500. The intermediate `.ToList()` inside the let does NOT rescue it because it sits inside the server-side projection lambda; EF tries to build SQL for the whole let before any materialization. (The sibling `let chkAppStatusme` at 3067 is a scalar `.Select(x=>x.ApprovedBy).FirstOrDefault()` with NO GroupBy and translates fine â€” only the GroupBy variant breaks, exactly as flagged.)

PORT REGRESSION: Legacy EF6 source at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs:3152-3154 has the byte-identical query (3 occurrences: 3152, 3393, 3651), where EF6's pipeline translated/client-evaluated grouped FirstOrDefault. EF Core 10 removed implicit client-eval of untranslatable fragments -> regression.

CORROBORATION: The developer ALREADY remediated this exact pattern in the sibling CreditSaleReturnNoteController.cs:345-356 by materializing `appUpdRows = db.ApprovalUpdates...ToList()` first, then doing `.GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(grp => grp.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).First()).Select(a => a.ApprovalStatus).ToList()` client-side via a Dictionary lookup. The PurchaseEntryExpense site at line 3064 (and its siblings in this file) were missed.

SEVERITY high: GetPurchaseEntryPaymentApproved grid runs on any non-empty PurchaseEntry-payment result set (requestpayment==true, Status==1); realistic data triggers it, not just edge data.

NOTE on the hunter's proposed fix: inserting `.AsEnumerable()` before `.GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy)` is NOT correct here. The let subquery is a correlated subquery INSIDE the server-side projection of `from a in db.PurchaseEntrys`; you cannot inject a client boundary into a correlated subquery that EF must still translate as part of the surrounding projection. The correct fix mirrors the legacy/CreditSaleReturnNote remediation: precompute the per-(TransEntry, ApprovedBy)-latest status lookup from a single materialized list, then reference it after the outer query is materialized.

method: PurchaseEntryExpense grid action #2 (from a in db.PurchaseEntrys)
breakClass: GroupBy-then-Select(g => g.OrderBy*/FirstOrDefault()) returning an ENTITY/row, used server-side (JOINed on g.FirstOrDefault(), or composed as a correlated 'latest-per-key' subquery in a non-materialized IQueryable). EF Core 10 cannot translate the grouped FirstOrDefault outside an aggregate context -> "could not be translated" runtime 500. Every finding has the identical shape in _archive/legacy-mvc5 where EF6 translated it, so each is a port regression.
why: Second copy of the same query block in this controller ('from a in db.PurchaseEntrys', not materialized). Same server-side correlated GroupBy-latest-per-approver returning entities -> untranslatable in EF Core 10 -> 500. Legacy has the matching let => port regression.
finalFix: Insert .AsEnumerable() after the correlated .Where(...) and before .GroupBy(...) at lines 3288-3290 so the translatable filter runs server-side and the GroupBy/OrderByDescending/FirstOrDefault grouping happens in memory, preserving EF6 output. Final form: let chkAppStatus = db.ApprovalUpdates.Where(x => x.TransEntry == a.PurchaseEntryId && x.Type == "PurchaseEntryPayment").AsEnumerable().GroupBy(l => l.ApprovedBy).Select(g => g.OrderByDescending(c => c.CreatedDate).FirstOrDefault()).Select(x => x.ApprovalStatus).ToList(). Apply the same fix to the identical still-unfixed copy at line 3064 (method GetPurchaseEntry).
reasoning:
Tried to refute but could not; both conditions hold. Method GetPurchaseEntryPayment (sig line 3185). (a) SERVER-SIDE: query v starts 'from a in db.PurchaseEntrys' with joins to db.Suppliers/db.PEPayments/etc (3274-3284), a composed IQueryable; let chkAppStatus is projected as chkAppStatus=chkAppStatus (3343) inside select new{...} enumerated by .ToList() at 3349 with NO materialization boundary before it. Inner subquery references outer var a (x.TransEntry==a.PurchaseEntryId) = correlated subquery in the non-materialized server projection; the inner .ToList() at 3290 is inside the per-row subquery and does not create an outer boundary; g is a db-side GroupBy not an IGrouping over materialized data, so the non-empty-group exemption does not apply. (b) WOULD 500 HIGH: GroupBy(key).Select(g=>g.OrderByDescending(...).FirstOrDefault()) returns a full ApprovalUpdate ENTITY per group (Models/Approval.cs:17-29); EF Core 10 only translates GroupBy with key+aggregate, so this latest-per-key entity pattern is untranslatable -> InvalidOperationException at query-compile time, before rows read, so it 500s on every hit regardless of data. Port regression confirmed: legacy _archive/legacy-mvc5/.../PurchaseEntryExpenseController.cs:3393-3395 is byte-identical and EF6 translated it.</parameter>
